title: Exactly-Once Transactional Consumer
description: Exactly-once processing using transactions/dedupe tables or stream guarantees; complex and tool-dependent.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Exactly-once transactional consumer requires message broker infrastructure plus transactional storage. Min ($100): Kafka-compatible service (AWS MSK t3.small ~$75) + PostgreSQL for deduplication table (db.t3.micro ~$25) for dev/low-throughput. Max ($5000): Production-grade streaming with Kafka cluster (3-node MSK m5.large ~$2500) + high-performance transactional database (db.r5.xlarge RDS ~$600) + distributed state store for checkpointing (Redis cluster ~$400) + network/data transfer costs (~$1500 for high-throughput scenarios). Costs scale significantly with message volume, retention requirements, and availability zones. Stream-native solutions like Kafka Streams or Flink add compute costs ($500-2000/month for managed services).
provenance.adoptionCost: Very high complexity pattern requiring deep distributed systems expertise. Implementation involves: (1) Understanding exactly-once semantics and idempotency guarantees (10-15 hours research/design), (2) Implementing transactional outbox or deduplication table logic with proper locking/isolation (20-30 hours), (3) Configuring message broker transactions (Kafka transactions, SQS FIFO with deduplication) (15-20 hours), (4) Building compensating logic for failure scenarios (15-25 hours), (5) Comprehensive testing of edge cases (duplicates, out-of-order, failures mid-transaction) (25-35 hours), (6) Performance tuning for transactional overhead (10-15 hours). Total: 95-140 hours at $75-100/hour blended rate = $7,125-$14,000. Using $7,500 as conservative estimate assuming some AI assistance but requiring senior engineering expertise. Pattern is tool-dependent and error-prone, requiring significant debugging and refinement.
provenance.licenseCost: Pattern can be implemented entirely with open-source tools (Apache Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis). While commercial distributions exist (Confluent Platform, Amazon MSK, Azure Event Hubs), these are typically consumption-based rather than license-based. Enterprise support contracts for Kafka (Confluent) range $25k-$100k+ annually but aren't required for implementation. The pattern itself is a design approach with no inherent licensing costs—organizations pay for infrastructure usage rather than licenses.

provides.reasoning: This is the core guarantee provided by this pattern. It ensures that messages/events are processed exactly once despite failures, retries, or duplicate deliveries, which is the primary value proposition of this pattern.
Implementing exactly-once processing inherently requires and results in idempotent message handling, where processing the same message multiple times produces the same result as processing it once.

requires.reasoning: Required for one implementation approach where deduplication tables and atomic commits are used to track processed messages within transactions, ensuring idempotent processing. This could be a relational database or any storage system supporting ACID transactions. This is optional because it represents one of two alternative implementation approaches.

Required for the alternative implementation approach where the streaming platform itself provides exactly-once semantics (e.g., Kafka with transactional producers/consumers, or Pulsar's exactly-once guarantees). This is more specific than a basic streaming platform and is optional because it represents one of two alternative implementation approaches.

Required for scenarios involving multiple transactional resources that need coordination (e.g., XA transactions, two-phase commit protocols) to maintain exactly-once semantics across distributed systems.

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern supports both audited and non-audited environments.
Pattern is GDPR-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is CCPA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is HIPAA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling and provider BAAs, not this pattern.
Pattern is SOX-agnostic; compliance depends on audit controls in the application layer, not this pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern implements transactional/dedup-table semantics for exactly-once delivery; incompatible with at-least-once (idempotency approach) or at-most-once (fire-and-forget).

requires_constraints.reason: Exactly-once transactional consumer pattern is cloud-agnostic and can be implemented on any platform.
This is a messaging/streaming architecture pattern that can be implemented in any language.
Exactly-once transactional consumers are typically used in backend systems that process async messages.
Pattern works with or without caching layers.
Exactly-once delivery requires async messaging infrastructure. Setting messaging_delivery_guarantee without enabling async_messaging is a logical contradiction.
Pattern typically requires distributed transaction coordination or deduplication tables.
Pattern supports both multi-tenant and single-tenant architectures.
Pattern can be used for both batch and real-time message processing.
Pattern works with both real-time streaming and batch messaging systems.
Pattern is agnostic to vector search usage.
Pattern can work with document stores or other database types for deduplication.
Pattern can use key-value stores or other databases for deduplication tracking.
Pattern is agnostic to graph database usage.
Pattern is agnostic to time-series database usage.
Pattern supports both OLTP and non-OLTP workloads with transactional guarantees.
Pattern can be used for OLAP workloads that require exactly-once processing.

requires_nfr.reason: Database dependency and transaction coordination limit max availability to 99.99%.
Transactional overhead and deduplication checks add minimum 50ms latency.
Transaction coordination and deduplication table lookups add tail latency.
Recovery requires transaction log replay and deduplication state verification.
Exactly-once semantics with transactions guarantee zero data loss.
Transactional processing and deduplication table contention limit throughput to 100K jobs/hour.
Transactional semantics inherently provide read-your-writes consistency.
Transaction-based processing ensures strict durability guarantees.

implementation_strategy.description: Strategy for achieving exactly-once semantics. Transactional outbox uses database transactions with outbox pattern; stream_native relies on platform guarantees (Kafka transactions); two_phase_commit uses distributed transactions; idempotent_consumer uses application-level deduplication.

idempotency_mechanism.description: How to track processed messages. Dedupe table stores message IDs in database; unique_constraint uses DB constraints; distributed_cache uses Redis/Memcached; stream_offset relies on consumer offset management.

transaction_isolation.description: Database transaction isolation level. Read_committed prevents dirty reads (fastest); repeatable_read prevents non-repeatable reads; serializable prevents phantom reads (slowest, strongest guarantee).

dedupe_retention_hours.description: How long to retain deduplication records in hours (1 hour to 1 year). Longer retention prevents duplicate processing of delayed/replayed messages but increases storage costs.

enable_dead_letter_queue.description: Whether to route failed messages to a dead letter queue after exhausting retries. Enables manual inspection and reprocessing of problematic messages.

warn_nfr.reason: Exactly-once transactional processing adds significant overhead (distributed transactions, deduplication table lookups, coordination). Below 100 jobs/hour, simpler at-least-once + idempotency is more cost-effective.
warn_nfr.message: exactly-once-transactional-consumer: peak_jobs_per_hour is {actual} (<100). The transactional overhead and complexity may outweigh benefits at this low volume. Consider simpler at-least-once delivery with application-level idempotency.
